comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my thirty-fourth published book and the fifth of my peak oil books for New Society Publishers. It first saw print ten years ago, and it's turned out to be one of the most durable of those works, despite being heavy on predictions, some of which doubtless seemed far-fetched at the time.  The revolution in military affairs I predicted, for example, is playing out very nearly to spec on the plains of eastern Ukraine while I write this, and even the corporate media (who are generally the last to notice, or at least talk about, the changes that matter) are beginning to talk about the twilight of America's global hegemony -- the central theme of this book and the central political fact of our time. If you want to see how well my predictions have done so far, and where we're headed next, you can order a copy here if you're in the US and here elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- as in, no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: The Trouble with Ideology, Update to Germanish Religion Book List, and Question on Storms
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To anyone on the Texas gulf coast, I hope you fare well in the storm coming overnight.

To Share: 1. I put together a fairly short post on "ideology," and whether it's something we all have, or a bad invention of the past few hundred years (spoiler alert: kinda both): https://jpowellrussell.com/#the_trouble_with_ideology
2. More directly related to MM, I finally got around to making sure all of the entries had a few thoughts to them and added a few new books to my list of "Books on Germanish Beliefs with Thoughts": https://jpowellrussell.com/#list_of_books_on_germanish_belief_with_thoughts

To Ask: There was a big thunderstorm going on while I did my SOP today (actually one of the very leading bands of Tropical Storm Beryl), and I noticed that the invocations seemed stronger than usual. I this normal? And whether normal or not, is it like more because of latent energy in the storm, my own psychological response to the storm, some combination, none of the above, or what?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.content:
Hi Jeff, I hope that you and yours are safe and sound.

I performed my SOP during storm front winds and rain this morning as well. I noticed a stronger, surging energy in Air and Water, and mostly typical for the rest. I wonder if that was similar with you or if it affected all directions.

Another thought: You follow a germanish pantheon. If you invoke in Thor's name (or Thunar's) and perhaps also use more of his symbols such as performing the sign of the hammer, then perhaps, during such a strong storm, that could have carried significantly more energy than normal on top of the elemental invokings.

Also, I've caught up with most of the last few magic mondays after the event. I had a thought on your question about the opposite of legibility. I first thought of the difference between consensus and dissensus (as coined by our host.) Concensus seems partly the aim of legibility and partly the product of it, most mandates, laws, and scientific pronouncements are supposedly a consensus whereby all other options have been discarded. Dissensus allows for and encourages multiple options, not only as tests, but as multi-fold solutions, rather than one size fits all. The second thought I had is that this links to one of my favorite concepts, complication vs complexity. A lego set and a car are complicated - many parts and animated functionality, but very legible, it can be taken apart documented and put back together. A body and a piece of land are complex, many parts and animated, but if you even try to focus on a part to observe it, you will affect the system, you can dissect it, but it will not go back together the same. Perhaps a word that could suffice for your concept would be personality. In it's literal sense - the quality of being a person. If our systems were acknowledged to have person-ality and were treated as complex and reactive, then we might start treating each other within the system more as persons. I think I may have muddled my thoughts a bit, but perhaps something in there is helpful.

Wishing you well.

comments.title: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
comments.content:
JMG - can you recommend any books or approaches to "the magic of the menstrual cycle"?
Anyone else?

I recently read "Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power" by Sjanie Hugo Wurlitzer and Alexandra Pope. My potted review follows: having read a good chunk of Cosmic Doctrine, I recognized the 4 season metaphor the authors apply to the 4 weeks of the menstrual cycle. It very rooted in acceptance of one's experience of altered states of mind/mood and so on. The main thing I found odd was the authors' emphasis on "the bleed" as the most sacred time. I can see how maybe they did this to counter balance our society's "go go go" mentality, but the actual seasons... all of them are kinda equally sacred, no?

So, spoiler alert, I'm male and have never had a period, so I'm curious what other books/schools of thought there may be out there about this.

Also, for the commentariate: if you've had a bunch of periods - did you notice a week to week difference? Did it track like seasons:
Spring - new ideas/plans
Summer - active, busy, getting everything done
Fall - celebrating completion, but also, being visited by one's inner critic
Winter - (the bleed) low-energy, rest, but also dreaming/visioning

Boston Bob

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content: Um, no, it's never something I've looked into, since I don't have a menstrual cycle, or for that matter a uterus to menstruate with. (I tend to focus my occult studies on things that I can do something with.) Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content:
Check out the book The Wise Wound by Penelope Shuttle and Peter Redgrove. Redgrove and Shuttle were British poets of the visionary type and novelists, and they wrote these nonfiction books together. You might also be interested in their second book and third books, Alchemy for Women: Personal Transformation Through Dreams and the Female Cycle and The Black Goddess and the Sixth Sense. Redgrove mostly wrote poetry and novels, but here he and Penelope explored occult themes. Also, the reality of how the man in a relationship is affected by his female partners menstrual cycle on the psychic level.

Their work was an influence on the group Current 93 and their album In Menstrual Night:
https://current931.bandcamp.com/album/in-menstrual-night-remaster

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content:

https://www.theguardian.com/books/2012/jan/20/lucid-dreamer-redgrove-neil-roberts-review


JPM

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content:
There are discussions of this in the Kalachakra Tantra and its commentaries; the outer aspect of Kalachakra is concerned with periodic time cycles in general, and the uterus has a particular importance to the Kalachakra initiation process. Uterine blood as a magical substance is also discussed in some of the tantras, for example, the Chakrasamvara Tantra. For any study of tantra, however, it's requisite that you first have initiation and a teacher. There's a great deal of oral teaching and commentary which you can only get from a living master.
There are also teachings and practices related to menstruation in certain Daoist lineages which have a tradition of Nudan (女丹), or female internal alchemy. It seems they're quite elaborate, but since I have a male body and lack a uterus, I've never studied any of that in depth.

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content:

JPM

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content: Yes! Who knows, maybe they borrowed some things :D

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content: I'm female and age 67, well past menopause. I was never pregnant so I had about as many cycles in my fertile time as a human woman can have. Short answer: I never noticed any week to week difference in my energy level or the kinds of activities or thoughts I had, even when I had my period, except when I was cramping on the first day. Caveat: unusually for a woman, I had no premenstrual symptoms such as mood changes that seem to affect many if not most women and I had an easy menopause, so I may be less sensitive to body and mind changes than most women.

replies.title: Re: Wild Power: Discover the Magic of Your Menstrual Cycle and Awaken the Feminine Path to Power
replies.content:
One thing that contemporary thought about the menstrual cycle forgets is that in earlier societies most women would not have had as many cycles as is common now. Depending on the nutrition in their culture women started later. Without birth control pregnancies would be more frequent. Breastfeeding tends to delay the return of cycles--once again this depends on nutrition because a certain level of fat in the body is required to trigger production of the necessary hormones. So, the idea that most adult women through human history went trooping off to the menstrual hut every 29 days is not accurate.

This is one of those topics that IMO attracts wishful thinking, dubious history and strong feelings.

Rita

comments.title: Magical Tools
comments.content:
I have a question concerning the ‘internalization’ of magical tools. I am almost done with reading your version of Levi’s Doctrine and Ritual, but I don’t recall reading there about using magical tools on the astral level. I’ve had some success recently with utilizing magical tools which I actually possess, but using them only in an astral form.

I know you emphasize workings on all levels simultaneously. But I am finding work with tools on an internal level fairly promising, if not only good practice.

Do you have any advice for adding the physical plane to my activities, without losing anything from the astral level?

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content: Interesting. I did that, and most of the old occult schools teach that, the other way around: make the tools on the material plane, get used to them, and then transfer that experience into imaginal/astral practice. You may be exploring new territory here, so please take careful notes of your experiences.

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content:
Original Questioner Here, JMG,

In relation to inner processes of magic, I notice how Levi juxtaposes the developed magical will with control of the passions. I like this — purity of will, but I have to admit I have neglected any sort of development of will; concentrating on what I hope is development of spirituality of the emotional center.

It’s interesting that Swedenborg equates the Will with Love. Do you think it necessary for a magician to develop the will? And what is the difference between the will and emotions, or the emotional center?

Thanks again.

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content: That's the great difference between the mage and the mystic. The mystic -- Swedenborg was one -- focuses on love. The mage focuses on will. To achieve anything beyond the basics in magic, as distinct from mysticism, you need to work on will. The great difference between the will and the emotions is that the will is free and the emotions are not. You feel about something the way you feel about it -- you don't have much choice in the matter. What you will, however, is up to you.

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content:
This would seem to imply that the mystic, by focusing on something that is outside of her control, is sacrificing the ability to advance as a mage; and that the mage, by focusing on the will, needs to learn to cut herself off from the source of the mystic's power, by learning to reduce the effects of the emotions.

Am I correct to think based on this that the highest reaches of magic and mysticism are inherently impossible to attain together? (And yes, I do plan to meditate on this!)

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content:
Yes. Imagine that you're trying to drive from Indianapolis to Denver. You could take I-74 and then I-80, or you could take I-70...but you can't drive one way without giving up your ability, as far as that trip is concerned, to drive the other.

Human souls are different. There are those for whom love is the supreme gift, and they're natural mystics; there are those for whom will is the supreme attainment, and they're natural mages; there are also those for whom wisdom is the supreme quest, and they're natural occultists. There are other paths, too, but those are the three that have been well developed in the Western tradition. Choose the route that works for you and follow it with everything you've got, and in due time you'll see the Rockies rising before you, luminous in the morning light.

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content: What if you have all 3 coursing through you in roughly equal measure? (It's quite noticeable as my kundalini awakening is finally ending that this happening in my case).

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content: Then you experiment and see which one you choose to follow up on during this life. Or you try to do all three, and see where it gets you.

replies.title: Re: Magical Tools
replies.content:
This brings up for me Gurdjieff’s description of “the Four Ways.”

The way of the Yogi, and the intellectual path.
The way of the Monk, and the emotional path.
The way of the so-called Fakir, utilizing the body.
Then also the Fourth Way, the way of the ‘sly man’, the path using all three — head, heart, and body.

Gurdjieff notes that any of these paths ultimately leads to the true Will — and immortality.

Egyptian Vulture

replies.title: Re: The 5 Rites and GD Magic
replies.content:
1) Yes, the Rites are quite commonly practiced in the GD tradition.

2) One complete rotation.

3) I don't recommend this. Instead, do the Rites first thing in the morning, right after you get up, and then do your other exercise routine later in the day. Then, once you've gotten to 21 reps of the rites in the morning, work up to another 21 just before going to bed. You'll get better results that way.

replies.title: Re: Ritual magic
replies.content:
One of the things that tolerably often annoys the bejesus out of me when I read English occult authors is the unconscious snobbery so many of them inherit from their obsessively class-conscious society. Dolores' insistence that everyone can spare a room for magical practice is one example; another example is the original set of OBOD Bardic grade lessons, which airily assumed that the student can afford a nice little cabin somewhere in the country as a retreat. It never occurs to most middle class English authors that most people don't have that kind of wealth to throw around -- alternatively, this may be one of the ways they surreptitiously pee around the borders of their teachings to say, "Only public school graduates welcome here, thank you very much!"

In the real world, that kind of thing is as pointless as it is unnecessary. This year marks the first time in my entire life that I've had a spare room I could set aside entirely for magical purposes (and I'd give just about anything for that not to be the case, but that's another issue). I've always done my practices in a space that has other uses -- usually in my bedroom, since that guarantees that I sleep in a regularly banished space. It's never hindered me in the least. By all means visualize the accoutrements Dolores asks you to have in whatever space you choose to practice in, and don't worry about it.

replies.title: Re: Ritual magic
replies.content:
To tag on a little here, well, in the first place, the temple DAN wants you to create is very much a Golden Dawn, (semi-masonic) temple. Such a space is definitely in keeping with the quasi-masonic Occult orders that were gaining popularity at the end of the nineteenth century. But if you think about what the operating space of a renaissance mage would have looked like, it probably wouldn't have been anything like that.
And, in the second place, it was only starting around that time that one could even begin to imagine having a dedicated ritual space set aside unless one was a priest and you were looking at their oratories. Otherwise, well, if anyone got a glimpse in the window or a left-open door into a room with pillars and elemental banners and all that, well, things wouldn't have looked all that good for one after that.

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Blessings; MOE Master Attunement; Catholic schism
replies.content:

2) Once he's excommunicated he's out of the Roman Catholic egregor, full stop. He doesn't have to worry about drawing on it! More generally, you have to align yourself with an egregor, and you have to be accepted by it.

3) Yes, there's a hierarchy. The egregors of all the sacramental Christian churches are "under," in a metaphorical sense, the overall egregor of sacramental Christianity. Within each church, in turn, there are sub-egregors -- for example, in the Roman church, the Benedictine order has its own sub-egregor, and each individual Benedictine monastery or nunnery has its own sub-sub-egregor. The same is true of individual churches, etc.

comments.title: Weed and magic
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I recently (at the ripe old age of 40) smoked my first joint since my youth.
Maybe I'm exaggerating, but with my dim druid eyes (I've been practicing from the druid magic manual for about a year), I felt that there were some magical effects that came with the high.

1) I noticed a shift in perception: when I went for a walk through the woods after smoking, I wouldn't have been surprised if a dwarf or troll or bigfoot had stepped out of the bushes. It just felt very different from everyday reality. Time passed more slowly and my field of vision became darker around the edges.

2) Heightened senses: When I did the protective sphere, my extremities tingled like crazy.

3) Visualization: It was incredibly easy to visualize things, the images were brighter and looked more substantial. Even when I closed my eyes, I had spontaneous visualizations. From landscapes to cartoon characters.

4) Prayer: I always pray to my guardian deity before going to bed. On this day, when I was high, I had the distinct impression that I was being listened to very attentively. It was almost like being received in an audience.

So I'm really interested: is there really a magical side to smoking weed? Does it perhaps influence the etheric body? Does it facilitate contact with the spirits? I knew that sadhus and dervishes smoked it, but before I tried it, I pretty much dismissed it as just another way to numb the senses and ignore reality. Now I'm not so sure.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Weed and magic
replies.content: In traditional Western occultism that and other drugs are seen as crutches.  At first, yes, you get this kind of result; then you start losing the ability to get results without the drug; and then the results fade out, leaving you worse than you started. I don't recommend using them.

replies.title: Nine Day Weeks and 26-hour days
replies.content:
A note on the nine-day week:

We can't have a nine-day week unless we also move to a 26-hour day. Here's why.

Remember that the naming of days depends on the planetary HOURS: Sunday is the day whose first hour is the hour of the Sun; Monday, the day whose first hour is of the Moon; and so on. The reason we cycle through all seven "planets" in naming our days is the mathematical fact that 7 and 24 are relatively prime; this means that when you run through a cycle of seven planets and then start over at the beginning, it takes a full week until you come back around to where you started.

But 9 and 24 are NOT relatively prime! If you allocate the hours in a 24-hour day among nine planets, you will come back around to where you started in the cycle in a mere three days. Starting on Sunday, the next day would be Saturday, then Monday, and then we'd be back to Sunday. (Make a table with 24 hours per day, and count out the hours with your finger in cycles of nine to see what I mean.)

The simplest solution would be to increase the number of hours in a day from 24 to 26. Nine and 26 are relatively prime, so once again we would be able to cycle through all nine planets before repeating.

QUESTION: Would there be any other occult effects from moving to a 26-hour day?

Hosea Tanatu

replies.title: Re: Nine Day Weeks and 26-hour days
replies.content: That would be interesting, because that would mean 13 hours to each day and night respectively, and there are alternative calendars that divide the year into 13 months of 28 days each, with one day left over outside of the week system (two on leap years). A shift from the rational (in the sense of multiply divisible) 12 hour day and 12 month year to the irrational (in the sense of prime and indivisible) 13 hour day and 13 month year would require a very interesting shift in human consciousness, but it's not impossible that the addition of Uranus and Neptune to the list of known planets might lead to that.

replies.title: Re: Nine Day Weeks and 26-hour days
replies.content: This is not quite right. There is another option, namely to move to a 32 hour day (16 hours during daytime and 16 during nighttime); and I've wondered for a while if this might work well paired with geomancy, and have come to suspect this is a more likely outcome for a few other reasons, including that a reworked zodiac with 16 signs seems to work surprisingly well, at least for me.

replies.title: Re: Nine Day Weeks and 26-hour days
replies.content:
Nine and 32 are relatively prime as well, so yes this would work too. I picked 26 hours because it's close to 24—trying to make the change less drastic—but you are absolutely right that it is not the only choice.

Hosea Tanatu

replies.title: Re: Nine Day Weeks and 26-hour days
replies.content:
Another possibility - and I think possibly a more likely one - would be to reduce the hours to 20, which is also relatively prime with 9.

Starting on Sunday would then yield a week that runs Sunday - Wednesday - (Neptune) - Saturday - Tuesday - Friday - Monday - (Uranus) - Thursday.

replies.title: Re: Symbols and egregores?

comments.title: MacGregor-Reid
comments.content:
Hi!

I've been doing some research on the Golden Dawn and the Druid Revival, have read some blog entries on this site and have been reading the Celtic Golden Dawn book. There is a lot of fascinating information here to appreciate and I have been enjoying the book! I recently read on your blog that George MacGregor-Reid was a member of the Golden Dawn. I recently read in The Les Druides - Les Societes Initiatiques Celtiques Contemporaines (The Druids - Contemporary Celtic Initiatory Societies) by Michel Raoult that George MacGregor-Reid was one of the original members of the G.D. I saw that MacGregor-Reid signed off a copy of the Book of Concealed Mystery as Frater A.I. of the Isis-Urania Temple. And, of course, as Adam Stout points out, there is the fact that MacGregor was added to his name which is interesting considering that it appears that Samuel MacGregor Mathers, one of the founders of the Order, had bestowed the MacGregor name to himself, Allan Bennett and Aleister Crowley at probably about the same time Reid started calling himself a MacGregor. My research seems to indicate that the MacGregor-Reid residence that was in Clapham, London was a Universalist Church, a Druid Grove and possibly a Golden Dawn Temple called the Nuada Temple. Do you know if this Nuada Temple actually existed? Some of the information I came across came from Ithel Colquhoun's Sword of Wisdom which is not always a reliable source and I have observed that MacGregor-Reid himself was not always truthful. And then there are the various and sundry books and articles on his great grandson Jamie Reid that also point to the GD connection. So it has been a bit of a rabbit hole. It seems George isn't on any of the lists of members of the GD though his secretary for his Druid Order, Charles Rosher, is well attested to have been a member of Isis-Urania. I was wondering how you came to know of MacGregor-Reid's involvement in the Golden Dawn? I can't seem to find anything entirely definitive although it seems clear he was involved in some way with the G.D. Order. Thank you very much for your time and your wealth of knowledge!

replies.title: Re: MacGregor-Reid

comments.title: finger magic
comments.content:
Several things here:

A quick reply to Robert Mathiesen whose question to me came after I went to bed last Monday:

"Was this Bishop Bang's Norske Hexeformularer og Magiske Opskrifter?" Yes, references to Norwegian spells that mention using one's fingers for spells and charms are in that compendium, Robert.

I also want to thank Robert for the beautiful poem you quoted, and note your thought of using Cat's Cradle designs as templates for magical finger-work, as I also wrote about. That does sound interesting!

Another thing along those lines that some of my Heathen friends are doing is knitting a flat, hexagonal piece that is textured to represent a labyrinth. When complete, this fabric is laid on a table and one meditatively follows the pattern with one's finger--'finger-walking' the labyrinth. It can be used for meditation, but seems to me that it also has potential as a tool for magical use. Just as one example, one could 'place' the aim or goal of a magical working at the center of the labyrinth, and use the finger-walking to raise and aim power toward the working. There could be 'stations' along the path where one pauses for step-by-step subsidiary magical workings along the way.

A question of mine that didn't get answered last week was whether Ogham Fews were ever used as a form of sign language or sign communication, with one's fingers representing the branches, laid against a straight edge like the edge of the hand or a table edge to represent the uprights? I guess it would be difficult to know whether that was ever done; I vaguely recall reading about it somewhere but don't know how accurate that was.

JMG, I am sorry to see that you are feeling blah, and hope you feel better soon!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: finger magic
replies.content:
You're very welcome, Winifred!

In Scott's novel, the poem is sung by Meg Merrilies, a gypsy woman who is using yarn of three different colors to spin a cord, the resultant length of which will foretell the course of young Mannering's life.

At times I have done something of the sort using rat-tail of three different colors bought from a fabric store, each color chosen to be appropriate for one of the three named components of the spell I am working. I knot them together at one end, then braid them until I reach the other end, naming each element of the spell as I put the cord of its color between the other two. When done, I knot the other end. Then I work the well-known nine knots charm on the braided cord ("By knot of one, this spell's begun. By knot of two, ..." etc.) The knotted cord should then be tied somewhere on the body of the person for whom the spell was made and worn until it feels like the spell has "taken."

Knot-magic and cord-magic is a useful thing to know how to do.

Also, from the books I cited it appears that there actually are dozens of historical "cats' cradles," each with its own design and rhythm, so one might pick and choose among them as seems most appropriate for the particular spellwork at hand.

There's a whole field of magic to explore here, and because of the patterns of three and nine, I suspect some form of it has/had Nordic roots.

replies.title: Re: finger magic
replies.content:
Before arthritis in my hands made it too hard for me, I used to make blessing cords in a similar way, especially for new babies but for other purposes as well. Depending on the purpose I galdored runes or sang songs of my own composing while braiding 3X3X3 varicolored strands of embroidery floss together, with the length equal to the approximate height of the recipient.

This relates to something that was mentioned in another message here, about the effect of magic on machines. Once I made a power-enhancing cord for a friend of mine, and laid it next to my computer and printer so I could email him to say it was done. Computer and printer refused to operate as long as the cord was there: a blank, black screen and total stillness. When I moved the cord away they worked normally, when I put it back, they didn’t.

Yes, cord magic is interesting!

Winifred

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
1) You've alluded to this before but could you talk more about Atlantis and the breeding programs it had?

There was a man who claimed to have Atlantean memories (which I don't think is likely true) but what he said was interesting. That it was a very oppressive highly hierarchal society with its own caste system, and ultimately a war broke out between factions.

This would match the breeding programs idea.

2) Was there any karmic link between what they were doing and their ultimate downfall?

3) Maybe not to the same scale (yet) but I see certain similar possibilities of that sort arising for humanity again in the next few hundred to thousand+ years, including the karmic consequences. Is this simply the cycle we play out, or are we trying to find an escape?

4) When we worship the sun in astrology, is that a lower order spirit than the solar logos, who is the actual big guy of our part of the universe?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) The guy may or may not have had memories but if occult tradition is anything to go by, he wasn't wrong. According to the traditional lore, Atlantean civilization was extremely hierarchical; their advanced technology, for example, was monopolized by the temples, above all the Sun Temples, and ordinary Atlanteans had no access to it at all. Children of every caste were watched carefully for signs of talent, and any gifted child, even from the lowest caste, would be whisked away to the temple for training and potential inclusion in the breeding program, because the priesthoods were technically outside the caste system.

You know that there were three separate Inundations (periods of flooding) before the last of Atlantis went under, right? According to tradition, again, the last one happened because a struggle broke out between the priesthoods (by then hopelessly corrupt) and the monarchy and its political-military establishment (by then equally corrupt). The priesthoods, despite their technology, lost, and triggered a doomsday weapon. The story has it that the island of Ruta, the last significant part of Atlantis above water, was a very large dormant volcano. The priesthoods had some way of extracting power from its heart -- some kind of geothermal system, maybe? -- and did something to it that caused a massive caldera eruption, leaving nothing behind but a very large undersea crater. (Letting enough seawater flow in and contact the underlying magna would do it.)

2) Remember that karma isn't simple tit-for-tat. The Atlanteans did plenty of things to pile up nasty karma for themselves, just as we're doing now, and that doubtless played a role in what happened to them, just as our collective karma will have a role in what happens to us.

3) I don't think modern industrial civilization has that long. We took a different route to self-destruction.

4) Er, astrologers don't worship the sun...

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
Is there a reason why their political system ended up this repressive? Or is it simply the general outcome of most civilisations to end up as oligarchies?

I ask because I believe political evolution is also among the possibilities we are creating and exploring, esp if we are to become lords of freedom.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
All civilizations up as oligarchies, for the simple reason that as a society becomes highly complex it has to centralize to keep from breaking down completely. That said, civilizations vary drastically as to how repressive and hierarchical they become -- some oligarchies are more repressive than other oligarchies. Atlantis happened to be very far over on the repressive end of the scale.

I think you're right about political evolution, btw, but that in itself requires us to experiment with many different possibilities, including bad ones.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
"Political evolution" is a fascinating topic! Especially when thinking about how ideas filter down from the higher planes and become a material reality.

Besides the obvious examples from the modern era, might we mark down Plato's political ideas in the "bad ones" column? Or do you think the proposed political system in The Republic was simply an elaborate spiritual allegory? (I actually think he was dead serious about it, while at the same time attempting to convey some of his spiritual teachings)

There's been some blogosphere chatter as of late pointing fingers at Plato, suggesting he was pretty much the OG inventor of totalitarianism. I think that's being just a bit hyperbolic, but I also realize his ideas have probably served as an inspiration for more than a few "well-meaning" tyrants. Funny enough, Plato seemed to walk back some of his more radical political proposals later in life; Laws calls for a much more pragmatic approach on how to properly establish a new state.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
Plato didn't invent much of anything politically. Like most Athenian aristocrats, he looked up to Sparta, and the institutions of his Republic were based on Spartan society, but with a privileged class of philosophers on top of it all.

Yes, those definitely belong in the category of really bad ideas. Did you know that Pol Pot drew as much of his inspiration from the Republic as from Marx?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
Plato's Callipolis would actually work fine for a society of machines, or perhaps intelligent insects -- a eusocial colony with workers, soldiers, and masterminds. The key is that each caste must embody only a single element of the tripartite soul, who then combine together in harmony. But Callipolis will never work for humans, and will always end disastrously, for the simple reason that all humans have all three elements. Meaning, the philosopher-king will have spirited and desiring elements, the guardians will have wisdom and desiring elements, and the workers will have wisdom and spirited elements. This will undermine the order, leading the philosopher-king to want to conquer territory and accumulate wealth and concubines (as rulers often behaved in history), the guardians and workers might get their own ideas and want to revolt to seize power for themselves, and the guardians in particular will chafe against their severe discipline.

So, there you have it, that's the fatal flaw in Plato's plan (when applied to humans at least). The Republic is still a great work, flaws notwithstanding; there's still much we can learn from it, even if some things must be taken with a grain of salt.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content: As someone tweeted recently, the best example of Plato's political system is the Islamic republic of Iran.

replies.title: Re: various
replies.content:
Ooooooo....

My stepsiblings may be in for some reproach, if it isn't happening already.

Two weekends ago I went back east to pay my respects to my deceased stepmother at a "memorial/celebration of life" the family had planned. It was held under a picnic shelter at a park next to lagoon, about a mile away from one of the Great Lakes.

The "memorial" turned out to be mostly a family reunion/picnic, with a brief couple of minutes where we all stood in a circle holding hands, and one stepsister said a prayer for and addressed thanks to her mother. Then a few of the family shared anecdotes of life/growing up with her (me included). The most moving of these was the reading of a letter she'd written to one of her sons long ago, musing on the beauty of the storm-driven surf crashing against the shore of an island in the Great Lake. Finally, we set out with her ashes to put them in the Great Lake as planned... until somebody in the party got cold feet.

"It's kind of a public beach out there." "There are going to be a lot of people." "It's illegal to dispose of human remains on a public beach like that." "Maybe we should spread her ashes here in the lake/lagoon instead," was the gist of the conversation.

There was no "beach" here at this lake/lagoon, just grass leading down to water weeds at the water's edge. The water was kind of green and covered with duckweed, it being summer and all. Long story short, my stepsister spread her mother's ashes in the shoreline water weeds. No one else in the party objected.

These were all people who had been raised by this woman as a single mother for years; she poured herself out to bring them up. They clearly loved her, my stepsister was quite broken up when she passed, and my stepbrother broke up crying when reading the letter. I only knew her from annual, brief visits for the last couple of decades, so I didn't feel like I was as qualified to say anything... but I was, and still am, rather appalled. The brevity, informality, and lack of care in the disposal of her ashes just doesn't sit well with me.

If it turns out to not sit well with my stepmother either, what can we do to make it up to her? Her ashes are now thoroughly distributed among the sedges and are irretrievable. No do-overs.

Thank you very much,

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: various
replies.content:
There's not much that you can do, other than apologizing to her.

For future reference, it's always wise to make sure of the details in advance. I think of Hunter S. Thompson, who wanted his ashes fired from a cannon -- and got it. Somebody obviously found, rented, and figured out how to use the cannon!

replies.title: Re: various

You can try to apologize, but I think there will be blowback regardless. Maybe not as much as openly disrespecting the dead, but a lack of respect or commitment is not good either.

No, that's a perfectly straightforward question -- you've narrowed it down sharply enough that I can answer it. You need to take up discursive meditation and make it a daily habit; you need to study sacred geometry in books until it's practically coming out your ears, and also get to work learning how to do geometrical constructions with pen, straightedge, and compasses, so you can draw constructions quickly, cleanly, and precisely, while contemplating what they mean.

You might find it useful to take up a basic protective ritual and practice it daily, simply because the astral and etheric environment is so cruddy these days, and daily divination will be good for developing your intuition, but those are secondary.

You should also study numerology, learn how to play a musical instrument, and become good enough at astrology to cast and interpret a chart. Numerology, harmonics, and astrology are the other three Pythagorean sciences, and they each cast useful light on sacred geometry.

If you do these things, and keep at them, ten years or so of hard work will get you into the upper tier of today's sacred geometers, and then you'll know enough to choose your own route to go further and reach attainments nobody's been able to touch since the Renaissance. I wish you all the best in this quest!

Hello JMG. I know that it is possible for occultists to speak with the dead

How do they do it? Specifically family, friends and loved ones.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not usually a good idea to do this unless the dead people in question were also occultists, or the kind of mystic that practices a lot of meditation. Most other people go through much of the after-death state in something not far from a dream state, and it's important to let them do it uninterrupted, as the more thoroughly they can process their experiences, the better their next life will be.

If the dead person is an occultist, they'll retain something like waking consciousness between lives, and they'll be aware of you if you're someone who mattered to them in life. Get into a meditative state and talk to them; the message will reach them. They can also get in touch with you, most easily when you're meditating. You can keep up a sort of "relationship by telephone" that way, which is frankly really helpful when you're dealing with a death.

As for using ritual to make the dead show up and do things for you, on the other hand, that's necromancy, and it's a very, very bad idea. The dead don't like to be bothered, and they can get back at you if you piss them off.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Consider a triangle made of three rods of equal length joined at the ends. The rods are each individual things, but they are also parts of a fourth thing, the triangle itself. The rods are Y, H, and V; the triangle is H final. The triangle is defined by the rods, but you can only assemble those three rods into a triangle of one specific shape and size. Meditate on that, using it as a template for what I said about the letters, and see if it makes more sense.

However, I noticed for most of Western Europe that the conjunction occurs in the 7th house and has an applying square with Mercury (6deg 49 seconds). Whereas in the opening lines you say there’s only a sextile with the sun.  Obviously we’re using different orbs for the square, unless you have another reason not to consider it.

replies.title: Re: Algol conjunction
replies.content: I use fairly tight orbs for mundane charts because I've found those to be useful in practice. Beyond 5 degrees I haven't seen much effect -- again, in mundane charts. Natal astrology's a whole different kettle of fish.

comments.title: Astral Attraction/repulsion love and hate
comments.content:
Last week there was a conversation about love and hate as how we humans experience attraction and repulsion on the astral plane.

You mentioned the Principle of Polarity and how movement towards one pole inevitably causes movemnt back towards the other.

Boston Bob asked if by "loving more and more each day" he was necessarily hating more and more each day somewhere else.

This idea that by loving something you inadvertently cause hate elsewhere piqued me and I have been pondering this concept since. I came up with the following, am I on the right track?:

The word 'love' doesnt always describe the same thing and the sort of love Boston Bob was describing is more akin to 'compassion' or 'equanimous love' than attractive love, and therefore is a state of balance rather than a pull towards a pole.

This lead me to consider attractive love and I suppose when one is 'in love', or loves a family member strongly, then one can feel hate towards those who you feel get between you, or who you perceive to hurt your loved one.

And if you love an idea, say a politial system, or *cough* those surrounding a certain virus, it can lead to, let's say strong negative feelings towards those with differing points of view.

Does this mean there was some weird sh*t going down on the astral plane in 2020 and 2021?

And finally a question: what causes these forces, this attraction and repulsion we experience as love and hate on the astral plane?

Thanks

FR

replies.title: Re: Astral Attraction/repulsion love and hate
replies.content:
Good. Now notice how often love, even wild passionate love, has an undercurrent of hate, and how often hate has an undercurrent of love. Emotions are complex things.

As for the cause of these currents, that's like asking "what causes events to happen on the material plane?" Lots of things...

comments.title: Question on angels
comments.content:
Hi, JMG,

Angels are very popular, to the point where I’ve seen pagans and witches say prayers to angels like Michael. But don’t angels serve Yahweh? Is there an issue with someone devoted to another deity saying a prayer to an angel, or do angels not care so much about what deity a person worships?

replies.title: Re: Question on angels
replies.content: There are many different theories about angels, and the one circulated by the mainstream religions is not the only option. The proof of the pudding is in the eating; if the witches and pagans you know get good results from invoking angels, that should tell you something.

comments.title: George King, Aetherius Society
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Any thoughts on George King, founder of the Aetherius Society in the 1950's in the UK? Any authenticity to his alleged communications through his invention of the "spiritual battery/radiator" machinery with Cosmic Masters and Intelligences from Venus, Mars, Saturn, etc., or the claim that his machinery helped prevent further wars and create periods of peace? His claim that Jesus and Buddha originated from Venus? Or his claim that there inheres a "Cosmic Council" on Saturn whose decisions affect our entire solar system and beyond? To me much of his work reeks of self-referentialty and narcissistic self-glorification and many of his followers idolatrous sycophants. Just a personal conjecture.

Many Thanks!

replies.title: Re: George King, Aetherius Society
replies.content: I haven't really looked into his work. There were plenty of people like that in the 1950s, trying to rewrite traditional occult teachings in order to put them into the mouths of flying saucer pilots -- that was George Adamski's schtick, for example, among many others. (What is it about contactees named George?  Adamski, Van Tassel, King, Hunt Williamson, and the list goes on.) If I ever have the spare time I'll compare their teachings against one another; since the basis of their claims in every case is "I got this from a saucer pilot," there'll be one set of conclusions to draw if they're all saying the same thing, and quite another if they're all saying something different!

comments.title: A Hypothesis of Trump's Glamour
comments.content:
A few days ago I staged a mental conversation with an imaginary severely TDS person-- in my 'daydream', when I came up to them they were ranting and raving loudly that Trump was The Most Existential Threat Ever.

Going through the conversation, I came to the realization that the 'glamour' that is on Trump, that I've sensed for years, is some kind of a mirror spell. When we look at Trump, we see ourselves:

- His supporters are populist, nationalist patriots, so that is what they think he is.
- I am populist and want what's best for the American people, so that's the impression I've been getting from Trump.
- My imaginary TDS person was a wokista who thought all working-class Americans were white male redneck bigots, and who went to BLM rallies/riots and burned things, some of the 'things' being Black people's stores, etc. So, in short, this imaginary person was a class-bigot, racist, tyrant, and desperately trying to hide this from themselves/others.

Just tossing this idea out onto the table, have what fun with it you will.

- Cicada Grove

comments.title: "Advanced" Druidry
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I have been practicing druidry for a a few years now. I completed the Dolmen Arch, and im now working on the Celtic Golden Dawn. Im enjoying having rigorous Druid systems to work with.

Recently, on a whim, I picked up a couple books about heathenry and rune lore. I was surprised by how in-depth and detailed they were on various concepts. I cant say ive had the same experience in druidry. It seems like most druid books ive come across are written for beginners. Those certainly have their place, but im in the mood for something deeper.

1. Do you have any suggestions and/or books for "advanced" druidical study specifically in the druid/celtic areas of spirituality? I very much enjoyed the Dolmen Arch and the Celtic Golden Dawn. Unfortunately, i only speak English...

replies.title: Re: "Advanced" Druidry
replies.content: Heathenry is much larger than the Druid Revival scene, and it's been enthusiastically busy going beyond the basics for years now. The Druid Revival was dead in the water until the end of the 20th century.  If you want to have a more detailed, rigorous Druid system you'll probably have to do the traditional thing and create it yourself.
